Alright, thank you everyone. Thank you for that introduction. Alright, so as you said, my name is Justin Brooke. And what I'm here to talk to you about today is what I believe is the future of online advertising. And I'm not talking about the future of online advertising like some sort of new age, woo woo, theories of things that I think are going to be the future. I'm talking about something that's happening right now. It's the biggest, hottest trend in media buying. It's replacing online, it's replacing media buying or display ads, banner ads, whatever you want to call it. And it's pretty much just swallowing that industry up. It's changing the whole landscape of that industry. And so the title of my presentation is called Confessions of a Native Advertiser. That's what I do. This thing is really squeaky. It's what I do, that's what I do for my clients. I've done a lot of different types of native advertising, a lot of different types of advertising. And to give you an idea of what native advertising is, there is display advertising or media buying, banner ads, and then there's content marketing. Native advertising is the evolution or the fusion of both of those. And when you combine them, it's more powerful than both of them on their own. And so that's what I'm talking about. And inside this presentation, I'm going to give you all kinds of screenshots, examples of success, best practices, the benefits of doing this. I mean, this presentation is packed with data and irrefutable proof that this is something that you should be doing, and this is something that can absolutely create a lot of ROI in your business. But before that, I want to direct your attention to the slide here. Does anybody know what that is? Raise your hand if you watch that. A couple of people, absolutely. On YouTube, it got 36 million views, and that's just on YouTube. It was streamed on the internet. It was on TV. That is Felix Baumgartner. And he jumped from space back down to Earth with just a parachute, and it was the highest free fall ever done. I think a Google exec just recently beat it, so I'm probably going to have to change the slide now. But if I flip over to the next slide here, you'll notice how many different Red Bull logos are in there. This was not content. This was an advertisement. And this is the only type of advertising that Red Bull does anymore. Same with GoPro, and a lot of companies are starting to do this. This is an example of native advertising. Now they're a big brand, and branding is a little bit more important to them, so there's not like a CTA or an offer there. We would typically have it. But this is an example of native advertising and how successful it can be. Does anybody know who these are? Show of hands. Come on, all right. Some ladies in here. Now I have to admit. I used to watch this show as well. This is Luke and Laura from General Hospital. I was a big fan of their son, Lucky Spencer. I used to have his hair cut back in middle school. And so they're from a soap opera called General Hospital, very successful soap opera. Does anybody in here know the story of how soap operas were created? Show of hands. Are the mics on? Can anybody want to go to a mic and tell the story? First one. If I remember right, wasn't it something created by Proctor and Gamble to sell to housewives when they were working at home, washing clothes and dishes? Absolutely. This, again, another form of content amplification, native advertising. Everybody's trying to name something to get in front of it. There's another new word being passed around, content vertizing. But it's basically, we would probably know it. There's a lot of direct response guys in this room. We would know this as using advertorials. And so this soap operas, they were invented as a piece of content to attract a certain audience so that they could sell soaps. That's why they're called soap operas. This show here, raise your hand if you've ever seen or heard of this show. Every single hand should go up here. Yeah, again, this is just another piece of content that was designed to sell you something. And it's so successful that the stars that are coming out of the show are eclipsing longtime A-list stars. Now you can buy the songs they're doing before they're even a star. Your little, people are out there in the millions buying the music of the losers of this show. That's just how successful this type of advertising is. Again, this isn't just big brand stuff. So I don't want you to think that I'm only going to be talking about big brand examples here. I found this example. This is from David Ogleveen. And today, this would be the equivalent of one of those list articles, 23 ways to improve your sales. But this was just the newspaper version of this. So what I'm talking to you about here today, native advertising, it's nothing new. It's not something I've invented or some crazy idea. It's something that's been working for decades. This is just the online version of it. And I'm going to be telling you the best practices and showing you examples of how people have implemented this and how they've taken it forward. So before I go forward, I want to tell you a little bit about myself. Like you said, I turned $60 into six figures. That wasn't immediate. When I got started in 2007, I was actually interning with Russell Brunson back there. He let me come out to Idaho. I worked with him for 30 days. My job was to watch. He's got this famous marketing library, a quarter million dollar marketing library, all the greatest stuff. My job was to go through this material I learned from Joe Polish and Jay Abraham. I'm just all the great Stan Kennedy stuff was in there. My job, I watched all that stuff and I had to write affiliate review articles so he could make his money back on this huge investment he made. So I got the education of a lifetime. It was still an unpaid internship though. So when I went home, I had an amazing knowledge, but I then had to apply it. I took $60, which was literally half of my electric bill because I didn't even have the $60. Paid half my electric bill, put the other half into an AdWords campaign, rolled that money 11 months in a row, I think it was. But basically the first month I made $140, paid my electric bill, did it again, made it again, rolled it over and over and over and over again. I had a six-figure business and ever since then, I saw that traffic, paid advertising was the thing that changed my life. It was the thing I was eating ramen noodles before paid traffic. After that, I'm going to Red Lobster. So I was like, man, this is the thing that I'm going to really focus on. Since then, I've driven over 12 million clicks for my clients. I've been able to land all the top people at the top of my Dream 100 list for clients. I've been very lucky, very blessed. And then the reason why I get these clients, these are some of the results that I get. My favorite one on here is the one down there at the bottom. That is a 30-day window for a client where we drove hundreds of thousands of clicks. The cost per website click was amazing. It was under 20 cents. If you add up the checkouts, that's the sales. There's over 800 sales there. Our cost per sale was in the 30s and 50 dollars. This client was averaging $160 per customer. This was huge for us. It was my one example of like 600% ROI at very high volumes. It's a big trophy for me. All right, so back to the presentation. The reason why native advertising works so well and why it's dominating other things is because it's like a Trojan horse. They don't really know that you're advertising to them. They see it as just a piece of content. And so they actually feel like it was their idea to buy from you. They feel like they discovered you instead of feeling like they were pitched by you. And it changes the whole dynamic of how this customer comes into your database. It changes their mindset. And so what it ends up doing is it ends up increasing your average revenue per order in your ROI because this person wasn't just buying very skeptically. They loved this article that you read and then they landed on your product and they thought it was all their idea through the whole way. So they end up buying more stuff from you. And when you take paid advertising, I got sick and tired of waiting around for Google. I was an SEO guy. I literally wrote a book about SEO. I got sick and tired of the penguins and the hummingbirds and the pandas. I saw the panda and penguin stuff coming about nine months before it hit and I jumped ship and I started going back to the paid ads. Instead of doing content and waiting for SEO to show up, that's what everybody writes blog posts for and articles for is hoping that they'll get to the number one in Google and then get some traffic. Don't wait around. Just use some paid ads. Drive it to your content. It's like putting gasoline on a fire. You instantly start getting that traffic in sales. This is not something that takes 90 days or six months to build up. This is something that takes as little as an hour and 38 minutes. That's one of my clients' records of the first sale we got from starting up a campaign. So now I want to show you some other examples. This is a very popular example of native advertising. This is a website called healthheadlines.com. Highly recommend you write that down. Study this website. This is a website I can almost recite back to you word for word at this point. I've studied it so hard. They've been doing this for years. This is where I kind of learned. Basically I was reverse engineering a product called force factor. Force factor is a supplement and they do tremendously well online. Rumors, I don't work for the company, but it's rumored that they spend like $50 million a year on advertising. I'm studying them and I use some spy tools and I found out all their traffic is coming from this healthheadlines.com. So I went to healthheadlines.com, they go back and started looking around and they come to find out that this is actually their website. Originally I thought, okay, well they just built a blog and started advertising their own stuff to it. If I show you, this is a website Russell was talking about, similar web. I use it all the time too. It's an amazing tool. They're getting 280,000 visits per month to that healthheadlines.com. That doesn't impress you. Let's take a look at howlifeworks.com. This is another website that all they do, their whole business model is native advertising. They don't sell products, they don't have a supplement or anything like that. What they do is they have this general interest blog that is just nothing but advertisements for companies. They get paid to create these blog posts. Let's take a look at their traffic. 2.4 million visits per month to this site and big companies are paying them to put ads on their blog so that they can run these native ads. Because the thing is, none of these companies really want to create a blog or write the content or anything like that. So howlifeworks came in and said, we'll do that for you. And now all their clients are building up this massive media source for them. So here's an example of one of my own ads to show you that I actually do this. I'm not just talking about this and to give you an idea of what it looks like. This is a Facebook ad. This one here, the copy, if you can all read it here, if you're using this popular tactic in your Facebook ads, you might be attracting the wrong kind of leads and customers. In this blog post, I show you the mistake people are making and how to easily fix it. So I never really tell them what I'm talking about. They know I'm talking about Facebook ads and they know I'm talking about mistakes. And any of you direct response marketing veterans will see that the headline that I'm using is based on a very popular, I think it's John Capel's headline, which was, are you making these mistakes with your English? So I just take things that are already successful and have already been proven to be winners and I modify them a little bit from my own audience and that's how I'm able to get great results. So that's one of the ads here and that one leads into, so it tells them this mistake and that mistake and that mistake. By the way, you should join my consulting program and I'll help you not make mistakes. That's basically how that native ad works. All right, so let me break it down a little bit for you here. My wife wanted me to create a picture of me break dancing, but I didn't think that that would work. All right, so here's the types of content that work best for doing this. I really, now it'll change per market. Your style is going to be a little bit different than my style. Not everybody likes bright pink shoes. So I like using common mistakes because I know that people, they don't like to make mistakes. Everybody is trying to move away from pain more so than they're trying to move towards pleasure. And that's why everybody wants to try and save money, but they don't necessarily want to try and make more money. They're trying to move away from pain. So that's why I like to use common mistakes the most. So just think about all the different mistakes that people in your market are making. If you sell kitchen knives, if you sell tomato plants, whatever you sell, what mistakes are they making? And then write an article that says, here's the top three mistakes people make with their kitchen knives or here's the top three mistakes tomato growers are making. And then tell them at the bottom, you lead them into what your solution is. The next one is a listicle. I hate this word, but for whatever it's worth, these work insanely well. They are the most popular type of native advertising. I'm not so sure that they're the most successful, but I know that they're the most popular type because they get very, very high click through rates. And it's basically an article that is a list. And you've all seen them, seven ways to do this and 23 ways to do that. That's basically what those articles are. And we saw David Ogluvy's article. That was basically a listicle just way before that word was ever invented. The other one, if you're in the B2B space, case studies. In the B2B space, case studies, that's like moss to a porch light. Everybody wants to know what is the real data? What's really working? How do I really get results? So you would create articles and there's tons of them out there. How Dropbox got its first 1,000 users. How so and so got this result. And they're very specific results and it's almost irresistible for people in your market to not click that because they want to know, how did they do this? I want to do that too. And then the fourth one there is just basic how to, old faithful, very reliable. You all know it works. Not really the most exciting form, but it works. Now this is something that I have spent over $30,000 of my own money, not my client's money. This is my own money that I spent testing to try and figure out how do I, okay, so I can use this content and get a visitor. I don't want a visitor. I don't want clicks. I want customers. And so I needed to figure out how do I, once I get them over here, convert a lot of these people to take the desired action that I want them to take. And so the average blog was really just very ineffective at doing this. And most blogs, they have the content over on the left hand side. They have a right hand sidebar that has all kinds of stuff in the sidebar. And then they usually have an opt-in box up at the top right. Well, I found that when you move the right hand sidebar over to the left, it actually works better. Think about Facebook, Amazon, most of the most popular websites you're using today, the left, the sidebar is on the left. All the action you take is over on the left hand side. And if you look at any of the eye tracking charts or heat scans or whatever, the way people view a website, there's two patterns that humans view a website. And the most popular is an F pattern. So they're looking across, down to the left, and then over a little bit. And so if you look here, based on that F pattern, you got a header opt-in box, and then you got your sidebar offer right there. They can't miss this stuff. It's the first stuff that they're seeing. The other pattern is a Z pattern. That's more of the skimmer. So the way this works is you're gonna have a header opt-in box. And you've probably seen this on websites. It's becoming incredibly popular. Tim Ferriss has it on his website now. Derek Halpern is a lot of guys that are out there that now have this. And then on the sidebar, I don't have anything else on the sidebar. I don't care about recent comments, recent articles. This is not designed to be a blog, like you just come in here and I'm blogging for the sake of blogging. This is designed to get that person to take an action. And so there's nothing else in there except for my sidebar offer. And you'll notice there's a difference in color. I went gray on the bottom and the sidebar. And the reason why I did that is I like to have two offers on there. Instead of just having one, which could get a little annoying to have that one thing over and over again, I like to have two. Because not everybody will go for the primary offer. I make the primary offer something very simple, very easy, usually some sort of a tool. It could be an ROI calculator. It could be a screen saver. It could be, for me, it's a spreadsheet, 202 traffic resources. And so anybody wants that. So it's a very easy object for them to opt into. And then the secondary offer is something that's a little bit more meaty. There's some people who are just looking for quick gadgets and whatnot. And then there's some people who are looking for serious education. They want something very meaty. So on the sidebar offer, I offer like a one hour long free video course. And so just appealing to the different senses. One is a tool, one is a free video course. Inside the content is where I'm linking to whatever may be more relevant to that piece of content. And so I'll usually have anywhere from two to three links. You don't really want to overdo it. This is, again, you're trying to be like a Trojan horse here. You're not trying to spam them or be very aggressive. So I'll have two to three in text links. So the article will have a couple of phrases in there that make sense. And those are hyperlinked to the landing page that I want to take them to. And then the bottom, again, same offer as the sidebar. Just right down there at the bottom of the blog post. So far, I have not been able to beat this layout. I'm sure one day I will or somebody else will beat this layout. But currently, that's the winner. I've been able to convert over 25% of the people who come to my article to taking a desired action. So instead of just having 5% or 10% of the people come to my blog and click through to something, 25%. And that number is really critical in making this work. So the first thing you should know is when I first started and when you first start, this isn't just going to automatically start working like bandits for you. You're going to have to tweak it a little bit, just like anything. But when you get this up to 25%, that's when the math really starts. I don't want to go into the math. But basically, once it's at 25%, it makes the ROI very easy to hit. So that's the goal that you want to be shooting for. And it's not 25% on any one of them, it's a combine. So the top is usually like 10 to 15% of people take that top one, 3 to 5% take the sidebar, 3 to 5 take the bottom, 1 to 3 in the content. And overall, it's designed, it's an environment that's designed to get them to take a desired action. I don't do any type of blogging, just a blog. I don't do daily blogging. I don't do weekly blogging. I blog, I don't even really call it blogging. I create a piece of content that's strategically designed to attract my customer and then attract them into an environment that I've strategically designed to turn them from a visitor into a lead. For me, it's all about leads. I'm a B2B business and agency. It's not very easy for me to just make a straight sale. But if I sold supplements, it wouldn't just be about lead, it would be about getting that sale. So here's the formula that I like to use. I start out with an NLP tactic called the yes set. Basically, you've probably seen this. I use it in my emails. I use it in my social posts. It's a very effective tactic to get people to want to read more and to want to pay attention to you. And basically how it works is if you're writing an article about Facebook ads, you might say, do you want to get more ROI out of your Facebook ads? Anybody who's coming to that article is going to answer yes to that question. You want to create a obvious yes answer, almost so obvious that they may think that you're a little obtuse with asking such a simple and obvious question. That's what you want. You want to get them in the yes frame of mind. You want to get them agreeing so that they're like, yes, this is what I want to be reading about. Very important to this tactic is getting them started with the NLP yes set. Now the other thing is not a formula that I created. It's an age old copywriting formula, problem agitate solution. So given you my example, you'll have to tweak it a little bit. I'm going to use tomato growing as an example here. So do you want to grow bigger, juicier tomatoes? Yes, absolutely. Okay, well problem. Okay, well the problem is you're getting these bugs on your plants or maybe you're growing them outside and you can't control when it's raining or when it's sunny or the squirrels are coming. So I'm just talking about what the problems are and then they start going into it. And what this problem causes is more problems. And so I go into all the different problems that that original problem starts causing for them. You spend money on these plants and now they're not growing and you told your wife that you're finally going to be a great gardener and you told your buddies that you're going to win this tomato growing tournament or whatever. I'm just making stuff up to give you guys an example. But you go with the problem, you agitate that problem and then you reveal a solution to them. And that solution is whatever you want them to do. Maybe you want them to buy a health supplement or your kitchen knives or your indoor tomato growing kit, whatever it is that you may want to do. This is what I do every single time with my articles. Now if you ever go and investigate my stuff, which most people end up doing afterwards, you'll see that I'm trying a lot of new stuff. So follow this formula, not the things you see me trying because a lot of the things that I'm trying are not working and I'm trying to find ways to beat this formula. But this is what's worked absolutely best for me. That's what I rely on when I really need to get ROI out of it. All right, so what ad networks should you use? These are the ad networks that I'm using. I believe, and Russell was talking about it, and maybe that's where I've got my belief from. I believe that there's this word traffic generation. Traffic isn't generated. Traffic is already there. There's already millions and billions of people online clicking things right now. That's what they want to do. That's why they're on the internet. They're there to click things. And so all you have to do is find out, you have to create something that attracts that eyeball and takes them from point A to point B. And so I don't look at it as traffic generation. I look at it more as moving that traffic a little bit, just changing the direction of that traffic. And so when I looked at Alexa.com, which is a website that ranks all the websites. And I looked at where is all the traffic at? And they have this button on Alexa where you can look at the top 500 websites. And the top top websites, Facebook, YouTube, Google, Twitter. So I'm just there. I'm just where the traffic is already at and creating little doodads and little pieces of content that takes them from where they're at, point A to point B where I want them to be. And then I've engineered that environment to take them where I actually want them to go. YouTube ads, I like this one the best because when I pull up my reports on what's getting me the best leads, the best buyers, the highest conversions, it's actually YouTube ads. And I think it's because they're watching a video first, so it's a more engaging type of content. They're probably consuming more of the content. Where the articles, they're probably skimming over them a little bit more. I don't really know exactly why it's working better, but YouTube ads, I'm getting the absolute best results from this. And there's just millions and millions of people watching YouTube ads. I really don't watch regular TV anymore. There's a Watch Later button on YouTube on all the different little videos. And I've got a Watch Later channel and a bunch of subscriptions. And that's what I watch. I'm really into kitchen knives, that's why I keep bringing that up. So I watch different chefs and whatever, talking about the high end kitchen knives and how to do those fancy cuts and stuff like that. So that's where I'm at. Now the next one that works really well is Facebook ads. And this one works so well because of all the targeting that you can do. I mean, if you want to, one of the things we're launching, we use HubSpot. We don't use Aweber infusion software, we use HubSpot. And we're creating HubSpot templates, HubSpot campaigns. And the way that we're marketing those is creating an article that one of the problems for HubSpot people is getting them onto a webinar. And so we created a template for them that helps them get people onto webinars more often. So we're creating a piece of content that tells them, here's the things you're doing wrong with HubSpot to get people on your webinars. And then inside that, we'll talk about the things that you need to do. You need to have your call to action here. You need to have these bullet points. You need to have these elements on your landing page. By the way, we happen to have a template for that click here to buy it. And so what I'm able to do in Facebook is I'm able to go and just target all the fans of HubSpot. I mean, how much more relevant could that be? I go to Facebook, I target all the fans of HubSpot, I show them an article about HubSpot, and I show them a tool that they can use to improve their results with it. Same thing with Twitter. Twitter, you can go, Twitter has a paid ads platform, a network just like Facebook. In fact, it's so similar to Facebook that Facebook has something called custom audiences, and Twitter has something called tailored audiences. They just can't call it the same thing for legal reasons, but Twitter ads is very, very competitive to Facebook ads. If any of you out here are running Facebook ads and are successful, the easiest way you can duplicate your success is just port that right over to Twitter, because it's very, very similar. But what you can do with Twitter that's a little bit better than Facebook ads, is you can target the followers of your competitors. I'm helping a WordPress theme right now, and they're trying to get to the number one spot in some marketplace for WordPress themes. And what we're doing is we're going around, and we're targeting all the fans of other WordPress themes, all the followers of other WordPress themes on Twitter. Twitter ads, very good. The other thing you can do with Twitter is you can target certain hashtags. For my hiring process, I'm looking for people who are typing in the hashtag. The hard work pays off, so it's HWPO. And so anybody using that hashtag is the type of person that I would love to hire. And so I'm using Twitter ads to target people who are using that hashtag. And so just like Google AdWords, the reason why Google AdWords works so well is it's an intent-based traffic. Somebody's typing in a keyword, and then you show an ad to them. You can do the same thing with Twitter. Somebody's mentioning a certain keyword on Twitter, and you can show an ad to them. The other two down there at the bottom, they're not at the bottom because they're any worse or they're the black sheep. They're actually the ways to really scale this. That's the hardcore native advertising platforms. I like the top three because I have a lot more flexibility in my options for targeting. And the bottom two are the ways that you could have your content on the bottom of USA Today, Wall Street Journal, Forbes.com. Basically all these giant online magazines, all these big top blogs that are out there at the bottom, they have these little ad units. They don't look like ad units. It just says popular on the web or other related articles. And those are actually paid placements. And the way that you get your stuff on those articles is using those networks. Tabula.com or content.ad. I'm gonna have a session here for questions here in a minute. So just write down your questions and we'll do the mic thing. So the next thing you need to know is budget and bidding. When I normally give this talk, it's usually to a little bit lesser experienced market, so the budgets are kind of dialed down. But you need to spend at least $10 per day. And the reason I say that is because anything less than that, it's just gonna be going very, very slow. It's gonna be very hard to get the results, get the data in to be able to start optimizing it. If you, instead of spending $10 per day for 10 days, spend the $1,000, collect all that data quickly. That way you can make decisions and then go on to spend more. Because most of the time when you're buying paid ads, that first investment that you make, that first $500 or $1,000 or whatever it is that you put in there, most of the time it's not gonna be a home run. I wish it was like that. But what happens is when you do that, you actually get the data of what was working and what was not working. And then the second investment you make is where the ROI comes from. And that's the biggest mistake that people are making with paid advertising, is they always make that first investment. They don't get a result and they're like, Facebook doesn't work. It's the second investment where people make money. All right, so my current recipe right now is $25 a day on four different networks. I'm on YouTube ads, I'm on Facebook ads, Twitter ads, and then retargeting. And for me, just spending $100 a day, which is $3,000 a month, has helped me build a million dollar per year agency. So it's been very well. My hardest problem, like I said, I'm hiring right now. I just can't be able to, that's my problem. So if any of you guys are great with hiring, I'd love to talk with you, give me some tips on that. And so yeah, that is basically sold out my agency. I don't have anything to pitch to you guys at the end of this. If you're waiting for an order form or a price drop or anything like that. It's not gonna happen, I'm completely full. Okay, so the next thing here is optimized CPM. This is, if you're using Facebook, most people automatically jump to the bidding type of CPC, cost per click. And that's okay in the beginning while you're trying to find out what ad is working and to really limit your costs and minimize the risks. But as soon as you have a good ad, something that's getting a 2%, 3% click through rate or higher, that's in the news feed. As soon as you have a good ad, you wanna switch over to CPM. Because on CPC, if you're paying 50 cents per click, then it doesn't matter, you're always gonna be paying 50 cents per click. But with CPM, you're paying $2 per 1,000 views of that ad. Well, in that 1,000 views, if I'm paying $2 per view and I get just 10 clicks out of that 1,000 views, then my click price is only 20 cents. So once you have a good ad, you will dramatically cut your costs by switching from CPC over to CPM. So in the beginning, start out with CPC, but look to move to CPM as soon as possible, and the difference on Facebook, they have something called Optimize CPM, which allows Facebook to optimize your ads to show to people who are more likely to click. Facebook, just like Google, they're collecting all this data on everybody they know, which ones of us like to click like on fan pages, they know who's clicking on ads and who's not clicking on ads. And so they will optimize your ads to show to people who are more likely to click. So use Optimize CPM, that's how I get the absolute cheapest clicks in the highest volume. The next thing is a lesson I learned from another great media buyer, Tommy Powers. And the concept is you want to bid high and budget low. And the reason why you want to bid high is because you want to buy the best traffic. There's a lot of people that what they do is they want cheap clicks and I get it, we got to cut our costs and we got to minimize our risk. But when you go into a network, any ad network, and you say that you're going to bid only five cents per click or only 10 cents per click, what you're actually doing is you're shooting yourself in the foot because you're identifying yourself right away as the nuisance client to the network. They don't want to give you the best traffic, they're going to give you the cheap traffic. So if you bid high, then they'll be giving you the best traffic and you can always manage your expenses and your costs by budgeting low. So if you only want to spend $10 per day, then set your budget to $10 per day, but set your bid as high as you can. I set my bid to $5. I'm trying to beat everybody else out. And now that I've said that a lot, I'm probably going to have to raise it to seven because other guys have jumped to five. So but a good bid for everybody to use and what I tell my employees to use just a starting bid is a dollar per click. Almost everyone should be able to get at least a dollar per click and that'll get you some decent traffic. The networks won't think you're the annoying guy at that level. All right. Next one is don't pause your ads. Every ad network, think about it. You know, would you like the guy who is constantly pausing, stopping and starting his campaigns or the guy who's just constantly going? It's a cash flow thing. You know, you don't want to be the nuisance client on a network, but if you need to stop things, if things aren't going well, just drop your bid really low so that you're not getting much traffic. So if things aren't going well, I just drop my budget down to like a dollar per day or $2 per day, whatever's comfortable to stop the bleeding so that I can change things and then start it back up. But I don't pause because I've seen so many times, I don't know what happens because I don't work for these ad networks, but sometimes you pause a campaign, it just never comes back to life. You know, I've had campaigns that were earning $40,000 a month for a client and we paused because we had to change something on the VSL and it just never came back alive. You know, rebuilding it, you know, something, I don't know, you lose your place in line or the network just doesn't like you anymore. For whatever reason, pausing campaigns is a no-no. So don't do that, just drop your bid to whatever is comfortable for you. All right. So who here would like to be able to know of a free tool that you can use to spy on the best native advertisements? All right, this is something you all can go to right away, no opt-in, nothing like that. This is leadboard.nativeadvertising.com. And what you'll be able to do is you'll be able to find and spy on the greatest native ads that are running today. And this is a great way to get headline ideas, content ideas. You know, again, just like Russell was talking about, don't recreate the wheel, don't reinvent things, go look at what's already worked, find a format and a template that would work well for you and just tweak it a little bit to make sure that it makes sense for your own market. So leadboard.nativeadvertising.com. They have a little bit of a weird interface here. Let's see if the laser pointer works. So if you want to change it so that you're seeing what works for your market instead of just all the different stuff, you actually have to click on these words and it creates a little drop down that you can change things. It wasn't obvious to me at first. I was clicking on that little magnifying lens. Nothing was happening. So that's a great resource. All right, so let's talk a little bit about why you should do this stuff here. The reason why I like native advertising so much is because it's like a Swiss Army knife. I don't just get sales. I don't just get leads, which that's great. That's what we're all here for. But I also get subscribers to my blog. I get people clicking like on my fan page. I get all kinds of market awareness. I get all kinds of building respect and loyalty. I mean, the reason why I'm able to get the top clients in my industry is because they see my content all the time and they're like, man, he really knows this stuff. And I'm always in front of their face with these different articles. And so the same thing will happen to you in your market. They'll see your articles all the time. And they're like, man, this person really knows this stuff. They have the best stuff in the market. I'm going to buy for them. And when they're ready to buy, there you'll be. You'll be right there in front of them. So you get all kinds of extra benefits. Just the other day, I think it was two days ago, actually, the CEO of HubSpot retweeted one of my Twitter ads. That was, it's amazing. You know, I mean, for me in the morning, I was like, yeah. So that's like some of the French benefits you get from using native advertising is there's all these other little things that will happen. And the types of followers that you get, I mean, just market influencers start following my Twitter account and my fan page because they see the stuff that's going on. And they want to see what else is going on. So it's like a Swiss army knife. The other thing is it separates you from all the other products. If all you are is the guy that's out there saying, hey, buy my stuff because it's bigger, it's pinker, it's louder, it's better, it's brighter, then really you're just like everybody else. This is an example at a grocery store. It's even worse online because there's so many more of us online and they can just click a button and go to another website. Well, when you use native advertising, when you use content, it gives them a reason to want to buy from you over the other guy because the other guys are saying, hey, buy my product, get 20% off today. Instead, you are explaining what the problem is and explaining the solution to the problem. They're like, oh, yeah, you know what, that's a really good idea. I want to buy from this guy. I like this guy. He helped me out. He led with value first. Or she. All right. The other thing is it decreases your costs. So the way that ad networks work is the higher your CTR, the higher your click-through rate is, the lower your costs will be because it's not a game where if I'm paying $5 per click and you're paying $1 per click, I immediately win. I win if my CTR is better because if I'm paying $5 per click and you're paying $1 per click but you're getting 100 more clicks than I'm getting per day, then you're actually making the network more. They still get their money, but they like the guy who's getting a higher click-through rate more than the other guys. It works on AdWords. It works on Facebook. It works on Twitter ads. It's just the way these networks are working today. So by having these blog posts, these constantly fresh ideas, these fresh angles with these exciting titles on them, it increases my click-through rate. And because it increases my click-through rate, drops down the cost per click, which drops down my cost per sale, which increases my ROI. Does everybody get that? You want to get a yes? Okay. All right. I didn't want to make sure I'm not lazing over everybody here. Let's see. It beats fatigue. I don't care how good you are at offers. I work with some of the biggest companies in the world and every offer will fatigue. Nothing lasts forever. And the way that you beat fatigue is by being able to constantly change what that front-end promotion is. You can keep selling the same product, but you've got to change the way that you're selling because eventually that message and that hook and that promo, you tap out all the people who are interested in that angle. So I got tired of rewriting my sales page over and over again. I saw what Health Headlines was doing. I was like, well, yeah, I could just have the blog kind of be the front-end. And so that was one way that I was able to change up the front-end over and over again and keep my offers hot. The next thing is it goes under the radar. They don't suspect that you're selling them. So you'll pick up a lot of customers that you normally would never be able to pick up. The people who are very marketing adverse, don't like high pitch sales. So maybe you have that aggressive funnel. Maybe you have the aggressive, hypey ads. And then you're also doing native advertising. So you're picking up the people who that stuff doesn't work for. We would all agree that there is a certain type of person who's very attracted to those hypey headlines. And then there's another type of person who completely hates that stuff. All the same product in two different ways. And native advertising is a way that you can market to all those people who are a little adverse to those other types of tactics. This is the big one for me. You're spreading value. I mean, you're leading with value. And that's the reason really why it increases your average revenue per order in your ROI is because you're giving first. I have this concept called the chocolate chip theory. And if you go into a bakery, the first thing they do is they give you a free sample of their chocolate chip cookies or their banana bread or whatever. And the reason why they do that is they know that if you love this cookie, you're not just going to be like, OK, thanks. I love the cookie. I'll come back tomorrow and get another one. No, you end up buying a whole box or a couple of boxes of the cookies. And so using this type of format, especially for all of us here that are info marketers, what better way to demonstrate how valuable our information is than to give away a little bit of a portion of it? And we all know it works when we use squeeze pages and lead gen pages and whatever. It actually works even better if you just give it to them in a blog post and then they discover your product and want to buy from you. And they'll still opt into your lead pages and stuff. ROI is still there. The biggest concern I get from people, from my clients when I'm telling them, you know, this is the only way I do things. If you want to do this way, then you know, you got to find another guy because this is all I do. And I tell them, you know, what they're concerned about is, well, I heard that if I add another step in front of my sales process, I'm going to reduce my conversions and it's going to make things harder. And I'll give them that. It does. Every time you add a step to something, usually it will reduce your conversions a little bit. It will add some more friction to the sales process. However, if you have a sales page, the average conversion rate on a sales page is 1%, 2%, maybe 0.5% for a lot of us out there. You know, so that means already 98% or more people are already leaving. You know, so when you're using a native ad or a blog post and content, whatever you want to call it, an advertorial, what you're doing is you're actually pre-selling them and you're qualifying them and the conversions actually raise on your sales page. So yeah, you're losing a little bit of the people on the front end, but the people that you're losing were the people you were already going to lose anyways. The people that were just going to immediately bounce, they're going to go to your article, they're going to immediately bounce, but those people who were maybe thinking about buying, who you couldn't convert with just a sales page, now all of a sudden they're interested. And so your conversions will actually rise on your sales page from using this stuff because you're pre-selling them, you're pre-qualifying them, the type of person. So if you go on the Facebook and you're targeting 45-year-old men who are interested in survival and then you take them to a blog post that talks about the top five prepper mistakes in the survival market and then you give them a solution, that person who then lands on the sales page, one they were targeted by demographic and psychographic in the network, but then they were filtered in the blog post and now they land on your sales page, it's the highest quality traffic I've ever seen. My conversion numbers are through the roof. So it absolutely increases your ROI, but I've got a little bit of a grudge with ROI. And my grudge with ROI, I'm not going to get religious here, I just thought this was a really cool picture and kind of demonstrates the point, is that there are so many companies out there who are so focused on ROI and profit and I get it. I want profit, I want sales, I mean that's why I'm in business, that's why I don't have a job, that's why I'm here, but there are some people who have gone to such extent that they're literally bringing the planet to its knees and we can't continue on this path for too much longer, otherwise there's not going to be a planet for us to enjoy anymore. So my mission is to kind of educate businesses and try and change the world through advertising to teach them that we don't have to make ROI the focus, it can absolutely be the symptom of what we're doing. ROI is still going to happen, profit is still going to happen, but what we're going to focus on is leading with value, gaining the respect and the trust and once we get the respect and the trust we're going to get the sale. No sale is ever going to happen without getting the value and the trust and the respect first. So you have to get that. So let ROI be the symptom, not the focus, that's my big message, that's why I'm really here today, this is what I'm really passionate about and that's it for me. So open up the question. Thank you. Any questions at all? Yeah, if you can go to the microphone if you have a question, I can't hear. Are the mics on? Yeah. I think you're on. With your perfect blog layout, how do you, do you put both, you said you had a primary offer and a secondary offer, are they both in the same block or were they separate? So in that left hand box, do you have like your easy report and then your higher level thing? Right, so there was three areas and then the primary offer is in the header up in box, that top area, the first thing they see on the page and then the secondary offer was over on the sidebar and underneath, so on the same page but in different locations. So that one offer per location. Right, yeah, thank you. I tried three offers and three offers didn't really work for me, but two offers worked very well. Hey, this is about Facebook ads. I run very successful CTR. Okay. Maybe four to five percent very easily. And as soon as I go to CPM, it just completely tanks. I mean, Facebook doesn't pull your custom audience when you switch to CPM. That whole thing that they optimize for the people that are going to get your ad, they stop pulling from your custom audience though, right? Like if you use a keyword, you know, Justin Brooke, you know, people that like you and then you go to CPM, they're not using that keyword anymore or something is going on. Let me ask you a question. When you switch over to CPM, are you also increasing the budget? No. No, you're not increasing any of the ad spend? No. When you switch over to CPM? No, no. I usually do $75 to $100 a day with a very, very, you know, targeted niche keyword that will convert. It's usually one to four percent and then using that keyword. It's actually astrology. That's the niche. And I still have everything the same, but then I go to optimize CPM. And another question. This is the hard thing is like everything that I said here is designed to work for most people, there's always going to be little scenarios. Now when you switch over to optimize CPM, are you changing in power editor that the bid is going to clicks versus, you know, it's normally default set to actions. Are you aware of that box in those check boxes? Have you ever? Why don't you tell me which one I should do? You want to do clicks. Okay. And so that's the one thing that I probably should have had on there is when you switch over to optimize CPM, you need to use power editor and inside power editor, there's four check boxes. You'll want to click the one for clicks, set it to a dollar per click because otherwise it's default set to actions, which is why it's killing your results because it's just optimizing for likes and engagement for not optimizing for clicks. Okay. So when you and then Facebook will still pull from your keyword. Yeah. It's going to have all the same targets. Everything is the same. That's not the reason why it's bombing. It's bombing because it's optimizing for actions instead of clicks. Okay. Thank you. All right. So with all of this stuff, you can type it into YouTube and find a video about it. How much would you use the same blog post? Till it stops working. And how I've been using that Facebook one eight months. I mean, I turn it off every once in a while and then I turn it back on, you know, but what you'll see is the average CTR pattern is it starts out a little low and it first gets started and then it goes high because it's a brand new ad that these people haven't seen before. And so they're clicking it. But after they see that ad and they see that ad and they see that ad, the CTR slowly starts trailing and then it usually takes a big tank after a certain point. And so what we do is when we start seeing that trail down, we try to catch it right before it takes the tank down and then we switch it. And so another thing that we do is we have like 10 of these things going at the same time. And then we use a tool called quiet.com and they allow us to rotate. I mean, you can do it by hand. You can go in there and pause and unpause, but this allows me to automate the rotation. So every day, only one of the 10 are showing. And so they're only seeing that same one like three times per month. What's that tool again? Quiet Q W A Y A dot com. There's a there's a bunch of them, but that's the one that I use. Can you address retargeting? It's something that's relatively new to me and I suspect there are others here who would like to understand that a little bit more. Yeah, I'm glad you brought that up. That's something I should add to my slides here. The one thing that we're doing, once we get them over to our blog articles or content, whatever, we're dropping retargeting pixels on them so that they see our ads all over the web. We see those offers that were on our pages inside Facebook, inside Twitter, around the web whenever they visit. And to explain what retargeting is, is you put a little piece of code on your website, it drops a cookie on that person's browser, basically tags them. And then based on that tag, when they go visiting the web, well, there's all the ad networks have deals together where they're sharing the information of that tag. And so because they are tagged, they start seeing the ads, your ads in the ad network instead of some other, unrelevant ad. That's how retargeting works. For retargeting, I use Google, I use a bunch of them. Right, and so it's a complicated question for me to answer because I like to have a lot of control. I mean, I'm very experienced with media buying. So I do things a little bit more advanced. I use a mixture of a bunch of different networks to achieve the result that I want. But if I just give you one good place to go to, it'd be retargeter.com or perfectaudience.com. Perfectaudience.com would be better more for the e-commerce guys. They don't like strong direct response stuff. So go more with retargeter.com. They're a little bit more direct response friendly. When you said that you didn't want to pause the campaign, I just wanted to clarify, are you saying to drop the budget or to drop the bidding amount? Say that again? Instead of pausing a campaign, are you dropping the budget for that campaign? Are you dropping the bid? The budget, yes, I'm sorry, not the bid, the budget. Drop the budget down. Okay, thanks. Yep. When you were doing Google AdWords, did you do a CPC or did you convert that to a CPM when you were doing AdWords? Google AdWords is pretty much a CPC unless you go over to their display network. And this was 2007, so it was a very different thing. But are you talking about that initial campaign that I did, the $60? Yeah, the first one you did. Yeah, so back then it was called Site Targeting. Now it's called the Google Display Network. And with Site Targeting, you could target a website. Basically, if you saw a website that had AdSense on it, you could target that website with your ad and without even having to ask them for permission or anything, just because they have the AdSense unit. So I was selling a video course about a software called Joomla, very similar to WordPress. And I targeted Joomla.org, the place where everybody goes to download this thing with one banner, pathetic $2 a day campaign, but it was, that one banner was so relevant to that audience that it worked. And it worked phenomenally well for a long time before I sold it. So are you doing any AdWords now? Do you think AdWords is just too expensive for most businesses? It depends on your model. It works very well for dentists, chiropractors. If you can get a very high customer value, if you can make a lot per customer, AdWords still works very, very well. If you're in the business where the margins are really low or your average lifetime value is $100, $200, it can be really hard because the cost per click is like $2, $3. There are ways to get your cost per click down, but that kind of is my answer. If you have a business where you're earning thousands per customer, you can still use Google AdWords very efficiently and probably will be the best thing for you to use actually. If you have a lesser lifetime value, you probably want to stick with Google Display Network or Facebook, something where you can get your click costs down much farther. Very good. Thank you. Yeah. Justin, you gave us two examples of the native content, the two websites. Yes. Do you have a system or a process of finding things like that so you can learn how somebody else is making all the mistakes? Yeah, I've got a whole set of tools that I use to reverse engineer what people are doing. You can really do 90% of what I can do with that similar web tool. If you pay for the pro version of that similar web tool, it's amazing. The only reason I don't is because I already had a bunch of tools that do the same thing, but basically that similar web tool will show you other websites like that website, where they're getting their traffic from, where their traffic's going to afterwards, the keywords they're ranking for, the demographics of that user. You'll find out a lot of information about those sites. Just using that tool alone, you should be able to find a lot of that stuff. Thank you. Yeah. Also, just knowing where people are advertising in your market, know where those top places are, call up some ad networks and ask them, and they'll be glad to tell you. Once you know where people are advertising, you can watch those spots to see who's advertising there, what kind of landing pages are they using. Yeah, I got it. Go ahead. Yeah, I came in a little bit late, so you may have covered this early on, but when you're doing your native content creation, do you have any suggestions on how to just come up with those ideas? Yeah, did you see the tool, leadboard.nativeadvertising.com? Yes. You did? So that's a great way to come up with the ideas. Another thing that I do is I just, I like to give credit where credit's due. I learned this from Marcus Sheridan, and he has this concept where he comes up with questions your market has. So we'll come up with 52 different questions your market has, and that gives you a blog post that you can create every week. You just write the answer to that question. So on the supplement side, doing what force factor is doing. Do you recommend setting up your own type site like health headlines, like what they're doing, or just piggybacking on other people's sites? You can do both. I like to create my own. That way I can control it and engineer the whole thing. That's what I like to do. All right, I think that's all the questions. Thank you very much for allowing me to be up here. Thank you. Thank you. Bye.
